transcript.transcript: This tape is an interview with Mr. Solomon Gomez. The interview took place on April 10, 1982 and deals with the Homedale area in relation to a Mexican-American in 1956. I shall trace the route of his legal migration to Idaho through his answers to my questions. Also present in the interview was Mr. Gomez's wife, Julia Gomez, who acted as a translator. I, Sol Gomez am the interviewer. The tape also includes various aspects of normal life for the Mexican-American in Homedale. Refer to the index for specifics on this subject. I came back in January. January 31, 1955. I came and working in Donut, Texas. I work in a ranch. What did you do at the ranch? I work in the ranch, in the tractor, and everything. Really? You have cows? Cows, yeah. I work in cows and horses and... You mean you farm? Yeah. Yeah. Did you... Where did you go after Texas? After Texas, I go, me and my brothers go to work in Idaho, in Homedale, Idaho. Working in Idaho, in Homedale, Idaho. Homedale? Yeah. How did you get from Texas to Idaho? I come back to... Somebody bring me to Idaho, you know, a guy in Texas. A relative? Yeah, a friend. Your friend? Yeah. Did you have a car? No. The friend got a truck, you know, and come back to Idaho. Did you hear in Texas, like, did you hear that Idaho had jobs there? Yeah, they said they had a whole lot of jobs in in Homedale and Marsing and Caldwell and and Nampa. Was your friend, was your friend from Idaho or? No, they're Texas. They're coming from the McAllen, Texas. Did you... And these men, I think they make a contract to the ranches in Idaho, in Homedale. Oh, you make... Yeah. Your friends? Yeah. No, they look like boss. Uh-huh. Yeah, they bring the people, you know, to Homedale. What, what does boss do? Well, they make a contract in the ranches you know. In Texas? No in Idaho. Oh okay and then they went out and looked for people who were working in Idaho. Yeah. Yeah, come and work in Idaho. That's where you got a job in Idaho. Yeah. Okay. I work in Homedale and Marsing and Nampa and Caldwell all around, all around this. I go working in Ontario Oregon too. Oh really? Did you, okay this man that contracted for Idaho, was it part of the Bracero program? No, no, this is, these people got a passport, you know, and tell them come and work here in Idaho. No more Braceros. Oh, okay. And the boss over there in the Homedale, you know, the big boss, his name's Joe. I don't know his last name. Joe? Spanish. Spanish? Yeah. Did he come, like, Did he go down to Texas and find people? No, this man lived over there in McAllen, Texas. Oh, did he? Yeah. And then he told people in Texas about the job. Yeah, they told me and my brothers. They come here to Idaho because a lot of jobs. Okay, when you got to Idaho, how did you know where to go to find a job? Because these men they got a contract and the ranches are ready to go there. Oh so he told you where to go? Yeah. And stuff like that. And Homedale I think they got a camp, you know, and they're leaving a lot of people in there, you know. And these, these, these men, they got a contract, you know, for I don't know how many renters, you know. And after the work in Idaho, I come in to California. Because I got a cousins in San Diego, California. Me and my brothers go over there and work in San Diego, California. Were you with some relatives in Idaho? Were there any residents in Idaho when you worked up there? Yeah, uncles. Yeah. When you were in Idaho, who did you stay with? Where did you stay? I stayed in a camp, in Homedale, I think about half a mile, you know, the Homedale. A lot of people living over there, you know, families and everything. How many people? You know... Maybe about a hundred or two hundred, I think. Did you notice the... Were there, like, were there black people there and, or just Mexicans? No, all Mexicans. All Mexicans, yeah. Coming from the Texas and Mexico. Really? Yeah. Did they come from different parts of Mexico or the same part? They come a lot of same part of Mexico sometimes. The general store, lot of people working over there. When you were visiting town, what can you tell me about the town? Living in camp, a lot of people working over there, and they got a store in there and everything, you know. Little store? Yeah, little store. You go buy cokes or buy something, you know. Uh-huh. Did you buy your own groceries, or...? They go buy the groceries, you know, in town. Oh, did they? In Homedale? Yeah, Homedale. How big was Homedale then? Not too big. Not too big. Bigger, smaller than my block? Yeah. And They came using gas and these came using wood, you know. Oh, you used... Really? Yeah. In the suits? Yeah. Was the... Were... How many, like, where did you live in? How many people were you... Me and my brothers, I think about four or five in one room. How big was the room? It was not too big, you know. Just a little bunk bed? Yeah, about four or five. Heated with a wood stove? Yeah, wood stove, no gas. Oh, wow. No gas. Wow. Okay. When you stayed in these campsites, did you maybe stay around your... Your brothers? Your brothers? Yeah. Me and my brothers working and... Sometimes go to town to buy clothes, you know, and come back and stay there. Uh-huh. How did you get into town? Somebody give a ride, maybe the boss or friends give a ride to town. Uh-huh. They go buy something. Yeah. When you stayed in... Well, where did you work at? Can you remember the name of the place? No, I no work at them. I no remember the place. I work in all around the Homedale and Marsing and Caldwell and Nampa and Ontario. Uh-huh. Yeah. Sometimes cross the border to Oregon to go work too. Oregon? Yeah. Did they just tell you where you were gonna work that day? Yeah, the boss would make a contract and he go workin' over there in the ranches. Oh, so it was all, the boss just told you where to go and that's where you went. And I think the big boss, the name Joe, this is the B-Boss to everybody over there. This is the, I think, the finest job for everybody. I think they call it the Asociación de Rancheros. The Association of Ranchers? Yeah, the Ranchers. Oh, okay. Yeah. Okay, And that was like all the ranches told his boss where his workers were. Yeah. Yeah. Okay. Did you notice like any Germans? No. No. I see Joe as a Spanish, you know. Coming from Spain or Basque or I don't know. These are Joe living right there in Homedale. Oh, really? They look a nice, nice man. Yeah, talk Spanish. Oh, did he? Yeah. I thought, well you said before that Joe lived in Texas too, did he have... No, this is another guy, the name, Manuel Lopez. Oh, okay. Yeah. These Manuel Lopez are living in McAllen. They be bald, they got a contract here the Rancheros Association, the name Joe. I don't know last name. Okay. Spanish. Yeah. Yeah. Spanish. Yeah. Yeah. Not Mexican? No, Spanish. Okay. Okay, like what did you do for, after you got off work? Like where did you go? Go to the show sometimes. Did you? Yeah. Do you remember the name of the theater or anything? I don't remember. Like, did you go to any bar or anything? Yeah, sometimes I go to the bar in there. I don't know the name of the bar. They call it Rock or something. I don't know. Yeah, right there by the Snake River. Were there any functions or anything like that that the whole community, the whole camp did for a period of time? No, in the camp sometimes Mexican dances. Oh, Mexican dances? Did they have a Mexican band? Yeah, a Mexican band too. Oh, really? Yeah. Did everybody go? A lot of people? Yeah, a lot of people go. Where was that usually at? You know, the big cabin or something? Yeah, sometimes making the dances right there in the camp, you know. And sometimes in the Homedale. But in a place or right there outside? They got a place in the Homedale, you know. I don't know the name there. The place. That's where they have the dances. Dances, yeah. Come a lot of people that Texas. All Texas. All Texas, yeah. Were they Mexican? Mexican in Texas, yeah. Coming from there. McAllen or Donut, Texas. San Juan and Endigul and Farr, Texas. Yeah, Allentown. Were there any other people like from Arizona? Yeah, I see Arizona and El Paso too. Did you notice that only Mexicans went to this dance or anything like that? Yeah, a lot of old Mexicans going to dances over there. No white people? No, I don't see. Were there, did you notice like, were there any people that tried to mingle the people of the Mexicans in the and that Homedale was it pretty separate the camp in Homedale? No. You mean the Mexicans from Homedale or just everybody from Homedale? Well just like there's Mexicans in the camp you may go with all the people in Homedale today. Yeah, they all basically got together over there. No, pero la gente de... Todos los mexicanos del campo no hacían lucha de... De ser amistad con los gente de Homedale. Oh, yeah. Yeah. A lot? Yeah, a lot. Yeah. But a long time ago, in Idaho, all people living over there, they come from Texas. Not too many born in Idaho. Oh, really? Yeah. I don't know. Maybe a little bit. It's probably most of the ones that he knew. Yeah, because, you know, when the winter time they go back to Texas, right now a lot of people stay here, you know. A long time ago not too many stayed over there. Oh, really? Yeah, and right now, I think they stay now, or live in an idyll. In Caldwell, or Nampa, or Homedale, you know, working all year, I think. But most of the people there... Yeah, and I don't stay over there, because they tell me it's too cold. Uh-huh. It's too cold, and I tell my brother, I better move to California. It's too cold? Yeah, they said it's too cold, there's no road, I don't know how many foot, you know, and I never see the snow. I never see and after the scare, me and my brother said, come and catch the bus to, to Homedale to Sanger, California, because I got a cousin over there, you know, and find a job for me and my brother. Uh-huh. Sanger, yeah. Where did you buy food? Did they have a... Yeah, they got one store in Homedale, they buy everything. Uh-huh. Yeah. And then you had that little market. Yeah, little market. I don't know the name of the market. And the camp? And the camp got a little bit look like bakery. And in Homedale they got a store for buying the groceries. What did you buy? Tortillas? Yeah, no, buy bread, meat, something for cooking, beans. And sometimes they go to Caldwell because it's a more big town. They go buy over there, sometimes buy clothes. It's more big. Did you have a... Did you go to church or anything like that? No I know the church over there. Did you know the priest or anything? No. No, I never went to church. Because I know car for going to church. Oh, I see. So you just stayed around the camp? Yeah. Did they have a church in the camp or anything like that? No. No. No. Okay. No. No. Were there any like, Was the police always out at the camp or did they have any trouble with the Mexicans? No, this, when I live over there, not too much trouble. Really? No, not too much trouble. Because, no, no more Brazil. These people come in the Mexico, the people in the Pasaport, too many come in there. The general Salam. The United States, United States I think they give a permit to Monterrey, this Nuevo Leon, you know, the state of Nuevo Leon, you know, and fees in the passport really easy. And me and my brother was going to fees in the passport, you know, and coming to the United States. Oh, the government in Mexico helped you? Yeah, in the United States too. Yeah, but that's how you got into the United States? I don't know if you remember Eisenhower or... Yeah. I think Eisenhower opened these people in Monterrey. They all really needed to get their passport, you know. In 1964, 1965. And that's how you got in? Yeah. That's how you got in. Yeah, come back and come and work in the United States. So there wasn't really that much trouble with the police or anything? No, no I never go in trouble with my brother either. Really? Did any of your yacht get in trouble there? No, because me and my brother are not drinking. Yeah. Never drink. And I go in trouble. Maybe a lot of people, I don't know, go, go cold well. So me and my brother stay in the job and the camp for this time working. When you, when you went to work, what did you do at work when you went? Did you pick grapes? No, working in a... I don't know they call it in English, they call it... Desaijar de Betabel. Big? What do you call it? It's big. Big, yeah. Yeah. They call it Desaijar de Betabel, you know. Uh-huh. Yeah. And what do you do? They clean up, you know, real good, you know, and pulling the betabel, but like this, you know, not too close because the betabel not go too big, you know, they need to... They need to... They call it in Spanish, the saijari. Yeah. You know, cutting in the middle, you know, pulling, but I don't know how many inches because put them too close, the bit, the bit, the bit will not go too big. Uh-huh. Yeah. Oh, I see. Okay, so you kind of cut off the sides of Yeah, yeah, yeah. And cleaned it all around. Was it in the ground? Yeah. And you pulled it out? Yeah. Not cutting, you know. Oh, okay. To the home, or what do you call it? The hoe? The hoe. Yeah, okay. Yeah. And did they have, well, like, how many people did they have working on one part? A lot or...? A lot. Sometimes go about, about 50 people working together, you know. Really? Yeah. Sometimes. Sometimes, 25. Really? Can you remember how much you got paid then or anything? Well, I don't know, remember, they pay for acres. See how many acres you make now. Oh, really? Yeah, I don't know, remember how much... Well, pay for how many acres you make out there, you know. Sometimes you make one acre out there, you know. But they need work. Yeah. Yeah, work. Was this only, like, for yourself? One acre would be for yourself? Yeah, sometimes, sometimes three or four work together though. Work together and make, make it more. Then they pay all of you? Yeah. One check? They make it one check to everybody. Oh yeah, that's kind of like how they do it, like with the garlic chicken. Yeah. They used to do that. Yeah, yeah. Oh, okay. Did they, did they pay you every, what? They pay every week. Every week, yeah. Did they always pay you? Sometimes when they finish the field, you know, when finish, you know, the boss they give it a check, you know, to the boss And after the boss they give it to me and my brother Joe gave you the check? Joe give it a check to to the man Lopez and Manuel Lopez give it to me and my brother. So Manuel Lopez is up there too? I don't know. Manuel Lopez, I don't know, maybe is in Texas. I don't know. No, but in Entitiempo. Yeah, Lopez is over there. Yeah, I worked for Manuel Lopez, you know. He's the boss, yeah. And he worked for Joe. Yeah, Manuel Lopez, I think, worked for Joe. Joe's the big boss, you know, the rancheros, Asociación de rancheros. Oh really? Yeah the beat boss, the writer in Homedale. Is that all you did was with the beets? Yeah, sometime in working in onions too. Onions? Yeah, clean up the onions. Where did you work with the beach? Was that around Homedale? Around Holmdale and Caldwell and Marsing and Nampa and a lot of small towns all around. Really? Yeah. Did you ever go into Boise? Real close to Boise too. Really? No. I go to Boise to go buy something over there in the stores. Uh-huh. Were the onions just around there too? Yeah, around the home there a lot of onions. Really? Yeah, in March. Uh-huh. Yeah. You said you went to Ontario. Yeah, sometimes I go across the border working in some place over there in Oregon too yeah what did you do there same same in the same thing Same, same, same thing. This? Yeah, working in the... In the... In the... Yeah. In the... Yeah, now, yeah, yeah. What did you do, like, okay, from the beginning of, like, when you went to work to when you got off work can you say what you did in between like where'd you go and... After the work they go take a shower and clean up and go cook because you need to cook on the hand and go get the woods, you know, start the stove because sometimes it's a little bit cold. Yeah. Yeah, a little bit cold. Like, okay, let's say you get up from bed in the morning. Yeah, and I start to cook and make you lunch. Okay. Yeah, make you lunch. And, and after I go, ready to go work. Uh-huh. And then, and then how do you get out to work? Is there a bus or...? No, this bus got a, got a truck, you know, and take everybody to work. Oh, really? Did you get your back or something? Yeah. You know, I need to go on the back. Sometimes it's cold. Yeah. Do you have a jacket? Yeah, I need heavy jackets sometimes. Was this in the summer? Going back sometimes cold. Yeah, yeah, heavy jackets. Was this in the summer? No, I started I think in April or May I started doing the Nevada Bay. And then it went through, through what, September? No, I don't know, not too many months working over there. Really? A couple? Yeah. And not a lot of people work in the poterios over there, too. Oh, really? Yeah. But I don't know, right now, maybe using the machine, you know. Yeah. Then they did it by hand? Yeah. A long time ago I think using by hand, but right now putting the machine. Really? Yeah. What, like, okay, you said you worked with your cousins and your brothers and your uncles? Yeah, my cousins and my brothers and my uncles. What was it like working with them? Was it okay? Yeah, it's okay, you know, working together. Yeah. It's alright. Okay. And, and my brothers, all of my brothers go back to Mexico and living over there in Mexico and come back. Still come back working here in California, here in Nevada. Right. Yeah. Nobody knows how. And I stay here and... Yeah. Yeah, I come back and working in California and started working in the South Pacific. I worked in Texas, New Mexico, Arizona, and California, Oregon, and Nevada and Utah. Okay, when you went into Homedale, were there white people in the community? I don't know, you know, long time ago. Tell me too. Me and my brothers, they say a lot of discrimination in Idaho. I think they got one store for go buy everything, you know, because they say a lot of discrimination in Idaho, I don't know right now. They had only one store for the Mexican people to go to. They got one store for go buy everything. Oh, just on the map? Yeah, I'm gonna say that. The Mexicans go over there and buy. But if, let's say you wanted to go to another store, could you go there? Yeah. No, they go buy in this store everybody. Oh, everybody, the white people too? Yeah, other white people too, yeah. But the Mexican go buy in this store, you know, the grocery. Yeah, they wouldn't let you go in another store? I don't know. They said these stores were for buying groceries, you know. Really? Yeah. Pero si querias ir a otro tienda te dejamos. I don't know if they go another store I don't know. Right. But they only told you to go to that one store. Yeah one store right here in Caldwell you know go buy good food. Did you run into any discrimination or anything out there? Well, I don't know, because sometimes you go, Sometimes you go hair cut and they tell you sorry. Really? Supposedly you go hair cut right there in the camp. I don't know why. I think because a long time ago The braceros, you know, the braceros that come in Mexico, maybe make too much trouble over there, you know. Yeah. Yeah. So after they come back, they pass a port, you know. These guys, they don't make too much trouble. Really? In Texas, you know, a lot of Mexicans, Americans, you know, in Texas, you know, too many. What about in the restaurants? Do you ever go in the restaurants? Yeah I go sometime in the cafe in Caldwell and sometimes tell sorry you know I can't serve you. Really? Did they give you any reason why they couldn't serve you anything? Yeah, they said sorry, you know. That doesn't make sense, no. They said I'm sorry, you know. Really? They got one cafe right there in the hotel and you go eat. Oh, really? Yeah. Did you see any black people around there or anything? No, I never see. Really? I never see the black people over there in Guantanamo. Oh, okay. Is there anything like can you remember anything that's offhand like what you can remember about Caldwell or anything? Yeah, I don't Caldwell, but I never see him. Not really? No. No, pero diga que de lo que te acuerdas es de Caldwell. Yeah, I go in Caldwell, you know. I see a lot of cowboys over there in Caldwell, you know. And I go to Caldwell because they got another camp over there in Mexico, you know, living in some place over there. Yeah. And I see a lot of... Look like apples over there too, and... Yeah, apple trees. Yeah. See any grapes or anything? No, I never see grades. Not good. I work in Idaho too. You like working in Idaho? Yeah. Is it a... They no querían... Go ahead. A song? Yeah. Yeah. But I've been in Idaho a long time. I go, oh, these braceros, you know, they come and they make a contract and come and work in Idaho. I heard they make too much trouble over there, you know, and the people, I think, they're scary. Oh, really? Yeah. They're scary. And then, what was that, before you came? Yeah, before I came over there, you know, because when I go over there no more braceros. Oh really? Yeah take it all the braceros yeah no more. They pass up for you know and I'm Mexican American they come in the Texas. Yeah you know, and a Mexican-American that come into Texas.

lines.line: # Introduction

> The interview begins with a brief introduction outlining the context of the conversation with Mr. Solomon Gomez, focusing on his migration journey from Mexico to Idaho and the Mexican-American community's experiences in Homedale.
** introduction, Solomon Gomez, migration, Mexican-American, Homedale, Idaho **
keywords: ** introduction, Solomon Gomez, migration, Mexican-American, Homedale, Idaho **

metadata.title: Introduction
metadata.synopsis: The interview begins with a brief introduction outlining the context of the conversation with Mr. Solomon Gomez, focusing on his migration journey from Mexico to Idaho and the Mexican-American community's experiences in Homedale.
metadata.keywords: introduction, Solomon Gomez, migration, Mexican-American, Homedale, Idaho

lines.line: # Journey to Idaho
> Mr. Gomez recounts his journey from Texas to Idaho, elaborating on his work at a ranch in Donut, Texas, before migrating to Idaho with his brothers, facilitated by a friend with a truck.
** journey, Texas, Idaho, migration, ranch work, transportation **
keywords: ** journey, Texas, Idaho, migration, ranch work, transportation **

metadata.title: Journey to Idaho
metadata.synopsis: Mr. Gomez recounts his journey from Texas to Idaho, elaborating on his work at a ranch in Donut, Texas, before migrating to Idaho with his brothers, facilitated by a friend with a truck.
metadata.keywords: journey, Texas, Idaho, migration, ranch work, transportation

lines.line: # Employment and Living Conditions in Idaho

> Discussion of contracts organized by influential figures like Manuel Lopez and Joe, which facilitated employment opportunities in Idaho. Mr. Gomez describes living in a camp with Mexican workers, emphasizing the community dynamics.
** employment, Idaho, Manuel Lopez, Joe, contracts, community, camp life **
keywords: ** employment, Idaho, Manuel Lopez, Joe, contracts, community, camp life **

metadata.title: Employment and Living Conditions in Idaho
metadata.synopsis: Discussion of contracts organized by influential figures like Manuel Lopez and Joe, which facilitated employment opportunities in Idaho. Mr. Gomez describes living in a camp with Mexican workers, emphasizing the community dynamics.
metadata.keywords: employment, Idaho, Manuel Lopez, Joe, contracts, community, camp life

lines.line: # Social Life and Entertainment
> Mr. Gomez shares insights into the entertainment and social activities in the camps, including Mexican dances and community gatherings, which helped maintain cultural traditions among the workers.
** social life, entertainment, Mexican dances, cultural traditions, community gatherings **
keywords: ** social life, entertainment, Mexican dances, cultural traditions, community gatherings **

metadata.title: Social Life and Entertainment
metadata.synopsis: Mr. Gomez shares insights into the entertainment and social activities in the camps, including Mexican dances and community gatherings, which helped maintain cultural traditions among the workers.
metadata.keywords: social life, entertainment, Mexican dances, cultural traditions, community gatherings

lines.line: # Work and Daily Routine

> A detailed account of Mr. Gomez’s daily work routine, including agricultural tasks such as thinning beets and working with onions. He describes the team-oriented nature of the work and payment methods.
** work routine, agriculture, beets, onions, teamwork, payment **
keywords: ** work routine, agriculture, beets, onions, teamwork, payment **

metadata.title: Work and Daily Routine
metadata.synopsis: A detailed account of Mr. Gomez’s daily work routine, including agricultural tasks such as thinning beets and working with onions. He describes the team-oriented nature of the work and payment methods.
metadata.keywords: work routine, agriculture, beets, onions, teamwork, payment

lines.line: # Interactions with the Wider Community
> Mr. Gomez discusses the broader community interactions, highlighting issues such as discrimination faced by Mexican workers and limited access to certain facilities compared to local residents.
** interactions, community, discrimination, Mexican workers, facilities access **
keywords: ** interactions, community, discrimination, Mexican workers, facilities access **

metadata.title: Interactions with the Wider Community

lines.line: # Conclusion and Reflections
> Concluding the interview, Mr. Gomez reflects on discrimination, past troubles with the Bracero program, and the evolving life for Mexican workers in Idaho, highlighting changes over time.
** conclusion, reflections, discrimination, Bracero program, changes, Mexican workers **

story.title: Gomez_Salomon_OH 2293 Side A
story.description: Recorded on April 10, 1982. Topic is Mexican-American immigration to Idaho.
